data.title: Why Strathcona Wants MEG Energy, And What It Will Pay For It
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise

We have previously covered MEG Energy (TSX:MEG:CA). We turned bullish on this in December 2024 and added another buy rating in March 2025. The stock was actually a good deal lower since our March article, until the news hit this morning.

Canadian operator Strathcona Resources (TSX:SCR:CA) has launched a hostile take-over bid for a fellow Calgary-based producer, MEG Energy, with an offer valued at more than $5.9 billion (USD $4.2 billion).

Strathcona is attempting to acquire all available MEG shares through a combination of equity and cash, it announced in a statement on Friday. It is offering $4.10 in cash per MEG share as well as 0.62 Strathcona shares per MEG share, which it says values the proposal at $23.27 per MEG share.

Strathcona says the deal is a 9.3% premium on the current value of MEG shares, which were trading up 16% on Friday morning.

Strathcona first approached Toronto-listed MEG on 28 April with a formal combination proposal after buying 23.4 million MEG shares — or 9.2% of all available shares — on the open market since the start of this year.

However, the MEG board told Strathcona on Tuesday that it was not interested in a merger.

Signaling it has no plans to back down, Strathcona said it intends to file a formal version of its $23.27-per-share offer with MEG “in the next two weeks”. The Strathcona board has already approved the proposal, which will be valid for at least 105 days once submitted.

Source: Upstream Online

Before we go further, we have to disclose that we have about equal stakes in MEG and Strathcona in our family accounts. Strathcona is the largest position in a separate account that we manage. So keep that in mind as we look to see why Strathcona is pursuing this. But first, let's look at how the company set itself to attack MEG Energy.

The Deal The Day Before

Strathcona pulled a Paramount Energy (POU:CA). For those not familiar, we would recommend you read our work on Paramount and why we made it our largest position for extremely low-risk 27% yield.

Strathcona sold 15% of their reserves for 33% of the enterprise value and immediately showed everyone exactly how undervalued they were.

In the first deal, Strathcona sold its Kwawa Asset to ARC Resources Ltd. for about $1.695 billion.

The second deal sees the Groundbirch Asset sold to Tourmaline Oil Corp. for $291.5 million in common Tourmaline Oil Corp. (TOU:CA) shares.

Finally, the Grande Prairie Asset has been sold for roughly $750 million. However, it is not stated which company the asset was sold to.

This moves makes up about 12% of Strathcona‘s 2024 operating earnings and 15% of its total value from proven reserves.

Source: CJDCTV

Granted that oilsands have higher break-evens than what they sold. But they also have far lower natural decline rates and that is big plus. They actually did this transaction yesterday (May 15, 2025). The end result is a debt-free balance sheet when these are completed.

So if MEG wants a hostile fight, Strathcona is ready. But why go after MEG in the first place?

The Real Reason

There is the old synergy line that we see in every single acquisition headline. If you believed the synergy logic, you would think that there should be just one company per sector in every country. Of course that is forgotten with companies that divest assets (didn't Strathcona just sell a bunch of assets?). But we think the reality might have to do with really, really boosting Strathcona's valuation. How would MEG do that? Well, Strathcona is a very low float company. Most of its shares are held by insiders. That is why, despite a very sizeable market capitalization, it is not included in most (if not all) passive ETFs on the TSX. Strathcona has been trying to fix this and it executed this flow-through transaction to increase what TSX views as the float.

Calgary, Alberta (January 31, 2025) – Strathcona Resources Ltd. (“Strathcona” or the “Company”) (TSX: SCR) today announced that Waterous Energy Fund Co-investment (Canadian) LP and Waterous Energy Fund Co-investment (International) LP (together, the “WEF Funds”), two of the limited partnerships comprising Waterous Energy Fund (“WEF”), have completed a share pass-through transaction resulting in the disposition of 24,010,576 common shares of Strathcona, representing approximately 11.2% of the issued and outstanding common shares of Strathcona, by the WEF Funds to their limited partners (the “Transaction”).

Following completion of the Transaction, the ownership of the issued and outstanding common shares of Strathcona by limited partnerships comprising WEF collectively decreased from approximately 90.8% to approximately 79.6%, resulting in public float of over 20%.

The Transaction was comprised of a series of reorganizational steps and the number of issued and outstanding shares of Strathcona remains the same following completion of the Transaction. The Transaction did not require shareholder approval.

Source: Strathcona

Perhaps this was not enough as we have not seen any buying by index funds. But picture this. If the two merge, the public float won't even remotely be a question. It will be more than sufficient. The combined company likely gets included into TSX 60 at some point. This should be a substantial passive flow move which creates a big valuation expansion tailwind. Strathcona also buys shares back to create even more pressure on the price down the line. So far, the company has not bought back a single share even during boom times as it has been worried about reducing the public float. But that could change with the combination and we believe, this is the big reason Strathcona is pursuing MEG.

What's A Good Price?

The initial offer is a such a lowball, that we actually checked our calendar to see if it was April 1. Sure, it is a "9% premium". But the price is way below its 2024 highs.

It is also at about 4.5X EV to EBITDA with the EBITDA being low due to oil at just $60 USD (versus over $100 USD in 2022). MEG is also in a position of immense strength. It has cleaned up its balance sheet and debt problems are an after-thought. The lack of big growth opportunities means that the free cash flow is going straight to the shareholders.

Reported free cash flow ("FCF") of $223 million ($0.86 per share) after funding $157 million of capital expenditures;

Returned $185 million of capital to shareholders:

Repurchased and cancelled 6.7 million shares, or 3% of total shares outstanding at December 31, 2024, for $159 million; and

Paid a quarterly cash dividend of $26 million, or $0.10 per share, on January 15, 2025;

Source: MEG Energy (emphasis ours)

So MEG is very well positioned to fight as well. Other suitors might be possible as well. Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ:CA) and Suncor Energy Inc. (SU:CA) both could step in here. Both could afford to pay more as their stocks are more expensive on valuation relative to Strathcona. We think at a minimum MEG would need a $30 offer and probably a $35 offer. As much as we would like Strathcona to get MEG for cheap (as we have a larger conflict of interest on that side), this price makes no sense to take the company off the sheets. Strathcona likely goes as high at least $30 while dialing up the cash portion of the offer. We are going to rate both companies as a Buy as we think both are undervalued. We will further note that a combination of the two alongside higher oil prices could create the potential for a big upside for the reasons mentioned above.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult with a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
